speaker: interviewer
text: Morris Rubenstein. You were in the middle of a story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we-- and we made the little, little rows, and then made one long, long one. And they put mines onto the-- and it-- it made the fish unconscious, like. And they came up. You know, and they came with big barrels, and we had to take it out with net and put it in the barrels. You know, and they took it away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't even get to eat the bread, nothing. You know? So every-- every day, they took us-- they took us to work, the-- the Germans. And a lot of times we went to work for some Ukrainians, Ukrainian police. And one-- one time, we went-- we went to work. And it was a-- a German, a commissar. And he told-- he told-- he told us-- he took us behind a house, or behind a barn, and he told us-- the men-- to turn around. And he told the women to take out everything what they brought. You know? See, they brought a lot of stuff, you know, to trade with the farmers for food. So they took it out. You know, and everything. And he said, well, now you can put it back in. And when you come back to the ghetto, don't tell the commissar is nice-- everything. Tell them he's mean. He beats you, he yells-- everything. So when you tell he is mean, you know, he gets-- he gets-- he gets good grades, you know, from the higher Germans. You know? So we went-- we went to work. And then after, you know, I went, you know, to the farmers. I begged some bread, and everything. And the farmers didn't turn you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Some of them used to give me a piece of bread. And some of them didn't. You know? But they didn't turn me in. They didn't turn me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the role of the Judenrat, once the ghetto was formed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Judenrat?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they like? What was the council like, once the ghetto was formed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Formed? Well, they were mean. That had-- they were mean. The police were mean. And everything, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there-- it was-- it was-- if one from the Judenrat, you know-- the German killed on the way someplace. And they killed a big doctor, too. When they ordered, you know, like, 10 or 15 Jews, you know, the Judenrat had to find the 10, 15 Jews and bring to the German. You know? They had responsibility, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one or two policemen, you know, they were real-- they were real mean. They were real-- they used to walk around with sticks in the ghetto. And a lot of times, I didn't go to work. And every time, they used to bring in dead Jews, you know, what they had to bury them and everything-- what the Germans killed, or some police.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw them bring in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw they bring in men. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you see that? Inside the ghetto? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside the ghetto. You know? One time it was a real-- a cripple, a Jewish guy. I don't know how he got out from the ghetto, but, you know, they killed him. And they brought him on a sled. They brought him into the ghetto, you know, so the other Jews would see him, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this stage, did you hear about deportations? That they were sending people to extermination camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from our ghetto, they didn't send them to the concentration camps. They killed them right there. You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: They had aktions-- they had actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actions? Yeah. They had actions. They had actions, you know, where they killed them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see some actions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't see no actions. If I would see the actions, I wouldn't be here. You know? So-- it was going on-- it was going on every day-- every day. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of times, I walked around in the ghetto. I didn't have nothing to eat, nothing to eat at all. You know? And I remember, you know, was a tree in the ghetto. It had a few cherries. You know, I crawled up and I got a few cherries. And this I survived, you know. I know one time I went out. And a farmer, a Ukrainian-- and I stayed there a few days. And I slept in the attic. And he just gave me bread to eat. And then he-- he told me, I have not myself to eat, you know? So then I went back into the ghetto, you know. So it was real, real, real, bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this what it was-- was going on-- was going on almost five year. And-- and after, you know, they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- they took me and a few other ones. Must have been about 10 or 15, maybe 20. Maybe more. And they told us to come into the-- to the Judenrat. And they round us up. And they took us to Lutsk. To-- you know, I jump from one to-- to another. But it was still going on before this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took you to Lutsk? The Germans or the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ukrainians. But the German, the [GERMAN]-- you know, he was a stern man. They call him [GERMAN], a commandant.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they round us up, I think, in three or two. And we were walking. And he-- he was coming over the what-- a coach with two horses. And he was sitting. And he told us to sing, you know, the-- the Jewish hymn. You know the Jewish national anthem, you know? [NON-ENGLISH]. And we start singing. And he was laughing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we were walking. And he was following us a little while, till we cross the little bridge. And we were walking, you know, with the-- the Ukrainian. They were on bikes. It was three of four Ukrainians. And we walked to Lutsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a walk?

speaker: interviewee
text: It could take about three, four hours walk. I forgot if we stopped or we didn't stop. I forgot. You know, Lutsk, we had to walk through it the ghetto, in Lutsk. And there was a little bridge, too, you know, over the ghetto. It was [NON-ENGLISH] bridge, they called it. A little iron bridge. We walked through.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while we walked, I run out. And I run away, you know? See, in Lutsk, the farmers had to go through the ghetto, because they would have to go the other way, I don't know how many miles it was, you know, to come around there. So I run-- I ran away. And I came to a house, the house there. And I don't know if the lady, she lived alone or more people lived there. But she did let me into the house. And right you come into the house, was like a shed. You know, before, like you walk in the front door, or the side over the shed. And she let me sleep in the shed, you know, on the ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I went out. I went out to the ghetto. And I met some-- a few boys there, Jewish boys. And a lady, you know, she was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was like a little restaurant. It was no restaurant, but she was cooking and everything. I forgot where I got the money-- and I got a soup. It was just beans, there was no bread. And then I came back there to sleep. And I stayed there a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed a week in the woman's shed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a week. Maybe longer than a week. I forgot already. Then I got sick. And right here, it was so swollen, you know, I couldn't chew. I couldn't eat. So she took me to a doctor. She walked with me. She was a young lady, you know, maybe 25-- you know. I think I must have been 14, 15 years old. Walked to the doctor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the doctor said I should come the next day, And he-- they have to get milk from a-- fresh milk, and then he give me a needle. It have to be mixed with something. So I came next day. He gave me the needle. And I came back. And I think it took a day or two. It start-- I start feeling better, and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I found another guy from our city, too. And he ran away, on the way there. I don't know if he ran away when we walk together, you know, because we couldn't look back to the other guys. We had to walk straight.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you ran away, though, no one came after you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I just ran away. No one. It's hard to explain, you know, how I ran, and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Weren't you afraid that something would happen to you when you'd run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid, yes. But you know, you don't think. You don't-- And I met the other guy. And we, the next morning, you know, we went out from the ghetto. And we walked-- and we walked home.

speaker: interviewer
text: To [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to the ghetto. Back to Rozyszcze.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rozyszcze.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the ghetto. And we waited till some people walked into the ghetto, some more Jewish people. And we walked in back to the ghetto. Soon I came-- when I came into the house, and they saw me, you know, what I stayed in our house. And right after, a Jewish policeman came from the Judenrat. And he took me. And he throwed me into jail, you know. And he told me, he said-- I'm going to have to go back the next day. So it was, but two or three, maybe four of us, you know, what we came back. And he took us back. And then they watched us so we wouldn't run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw some more guys from our city. And they took us to camp. And it was a camp, you know, like a barn. And it was beds. You know? It was one and two, you know, in the-- in the camp. They took you outside of the ghetto? Or was this inside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Outside the ghetto was the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a workings camp, arbeitslager.

speaker: interviewer
text: The name of it was arbeitslager?

speaker: interviewee
text: Arbeitslager. You know, like a slave camp. And it was a Jewish a policeman there, one or two. I forgot already. And every morning, like about 5:30, 6 o'clock, they used to come-- up! Up! Up! And he had a stick he used to hit us to get up. And we walked to the-- we walked into the ghetto. And we walked into the Judenrat, to the Jewish council. And he gave us little, little pieces of bread, you know, And we went to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one morning I came, and it was a line. And he gave me a little piece of bread, the policeman. And I eat it up right away. And he asked me, did you get a piece of bread yet? I said, no. So he gave me the other piece of bread--I ate up. So the guy behind said, he got already a piece of bread. So he slapped me, you know, the policeman-- the Jewish policeman, you know? I know his name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH]. I think he was in our ghetto policeman, too. I don't know how he got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went to work. And there was real Gestapos-- SS. And on the way to walk in, it was fenced in.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the camp, now?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To work for the Germans. Yeah? So we walked in. So we walked. I don't know if I was wearing a hat or not. And a Gestapo walked through. And he told me to take the hat off. But he said in German, [GERMAN]. I didn't know what a [GERMAN] was. And so the guy behind me said, take the hat off. I guess he knew German.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the German slapped me so hard. You know-- I-- I remember I almost went over.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we walked in, and a Gestapo came out. And he said, Juden, you're here to work. If you can't work, we'll-- we'll kill you. And the same thing we do to the Germans. If they can fight, they fight. If not, if they start running away, we kill them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, the same thing we'll do to you. We're not going to feed you. If you can work, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were working. We loaded trucks, you know, canned food and other things on the trucks. Take the work. We worked the whole day along.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had to sweep. And we had to move furniture up and down there. And then we came-- and then they had to give us a-- a [GERMAN]. How you say [GERMAN]? You know, a paper so we allowed to leave? So on the way back, we walked alone. They know we couldn't run away. You know? Where are we going to go? So we used to stop. And a-- and a lot of the-- the Gentiles, or the Ukrainians, Polacks-- they used to take the Jewish houses, if they didn't live there, you know, apart. And they used to look for gold, silver, you know. So some houses were already broken apart. So we used to break out the wood. And we used to make bundles. And we brought it into the ghetto. And we used to sell it, you know, for so much. And we used to buy food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I forgot how long I was in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the work camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the work camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, what was the name of the work camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I-- I-- I don't even know that. I forgot the name. You know? I know it was-- it was [NON-ENGLISH], or [NON-ENGLISH]. And you know what? I forgot even the streets in Lutsk. And I knew Lutsk good. I used to go there a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lutsk, there was a resistance movement-- and I think led by someone by the name of Joel Szczerbat.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I didn't even know about it? When I was reading it, I didn't even know about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were not familiar.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not familiar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any resistance movements in your ghetto that you were aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I'm not aware of it, even. I'm not aware-- it wasn't-- if there was a resistance or not. You know. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just remember in the-- and I thought I was left-- I was the only one left. But I heard one more, another one was left. He's in Belgium, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, I came back there, to the camp. So they give us-- it was a little soup. I don't know what it was. Beets or what? But it was-- it was just like water. No bread. No nothing. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I know, next was a German-- Germans were staying there. And they had a thing on top, you know, what used to be watchman. A German used to watch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right near the camp, near the working camp, near the slave camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which is close to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It was close to the ghetto, too, you know. And this I remember. And they said, you know, the-- the camp was-- the fences were electrocuted. You know, if you run, you get electrocuted. I don't know if it was the true or not. And then the next day, we went to work. And I worked. And it was a Ukrainian place with, where the Ukrainian police was. They had to give us a little soup. You know, and we loaded trucks. I forgot the potatoes or rye, or what we worked all day. Then we came back. Sometimes we brought a little bundle wood what we sold. And we used to buy some food in the ghetto. A lot of them, they used to bake bread and everything. And I remember one time, I think it could be some day what we didn't go to work. So a few of us, we walked into the ghetto. I don't know how we walked into the ghetto, how we went out from the camp. I forgot already. And I went there, you know, not just me-- a few other guys asking for bread from the Jewish. They bake bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they didn't give us no bread. But they give us the-- but they cut the hard pieces from the bread around, you know? So I saw a farmer-- he was selling cherries. So I went over to the farmer and asked him if I can have some bread. So he-- he sold, you know, by the cup. So he took a cup. And he said, here. So I couldn't put in. So then I had a shirt-- so he put it in. So I had the cherries and the little piece of bread. And I walk back into the ghetto. But I really don't know how. A lot of times, we used to go into the ghetto. And I saw a lot of guys they brought from our city-- boys, young boys my age, some of them elder-- or they saw me. And-- and the next day or two, I went out and I didn't see them. You know? I bet they killed them already. And I saw the-- And then--one time I came back to the camp. And I was real down. And they told me, you know a guy? You know, I knew the guy. I went to cheder with him. His name was Isaac. They said he ran away from the camp. So I was sleeping-- you know, I was shaken. Because they said this thing was electrocuted. How can I run away? And the door, the gate, you know, was just like this, you know, to walk out. I don't know if it was the same night, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got up in the morning. It must have been about 3 o'clock or 3:30. And I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The--

speaker: interviewer
text: We are in the middle of tape three. And we had to pause because a light blew out in the middle of the interview. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The [GERMAN], the mass killing trucks, were in your region during this time period-- '41 to '43. Did you see any [GERMAN]?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. You mean, where they used to kill? I didn't. If I would see them, I wouldn't be here. You know? I wouldn't be here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think, before the light blew out, you were in the middle of a story. You were thinking about leaving. But you were afraid of the electric--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The fence.

speaker: interviewer
text: --electrocuted fence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Everything. But one time, I got up in the morning. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I just looked up. And the sun was just coming out. And I just went through the fence. And I start walking. I took off the Star of David from the front, the yellow Star of David from the back. And I started walking.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think the German, you know, he was up there. You know? I don't know if he was watching the camp or he was watching, you know? They had there, you know, the trucks. And there was some artillery, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I start walking. And I walked away. And I walked by the side. And when I saw a German driving through with a truck or car, I ran and I hid.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, it was like-- it was big trees around. And there were some houses. You know? Or I-- I-- I jumped. I lay down in the ditch. You know, they drove through. And then I came-- not far was a little city. They called [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was-- it was a woods. It was a long woods. It wasn't wide, but long. And I walked with the wood. And then some Jewish people [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] live there. You know, I thought they were still there. But they weren't there. I guess they-- I don't know where they went, they got them into the ghetto or not. And while I went there, so I walked into a few places. It was farms. And I asked them for some food. And some of them give me some food. And I-- and I walked. It was five miles from Rozyszcze. And I walked. And I wait with some more people, walking, and I put in back the Star of David. And I walked into the ghetto. And I walked into the ghetto. I forgot. I think I slept over someplace. I think in the morning I walked into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saw one from the Judenrat. He was a rabbi [NON-ENGLISH], but he belonged to the Judenrat. And I asked him what shall I do? Whatever. And he told me, I can't tell you what to do anymore. And I walked into our house-- there were just a few people still there. And a neighbor girl, you know, she walked out. She saw me. She start talking, Moishe, Moishe, how did you get away? And she gave me some soup,

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember. And I sit down. And I ate the soup. And I went into our house, to the shop. And I took a rake. And I walked up the hill. And I forgot if I took out the Star of David or not. And the ghetto was already around with Ukrainian police. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I walked up and I walked through there, through the-- the property, there, you know, it was a farm. It was a big farm. There were two, three Ukrainians. They start running to me with a rifle. Where are you going? So I said, don't you know? I'm going to work. So that Ukrainian said, don't you know? Let him go. He's our brother. I know the Ukrainians from before. I used to graze some cows with them. And-- This is the end of tape three. And we'll continue the story with Morris Rubenstein.
